key_points: Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor
Electric current is measured using an ammeter and its SI unit is ampere
Potential difference is the work done to move a unit charge between two points
Ohm’s law states that current is directly proportional to voltage when temperature remains constant
Resistance is the opposition offered by a conductor to the flow of electric current
Resistors can be connected in series or parallel in an electric circuit
Electric power is the rate at which electrical energy is consumed
examples: A current flows in a wire when it is connected to a battery forming a closed circuit
An electric bulb glows when current passes through its filament
Resistors connected in series increase the total resistance of the circuit
Resistors connected in parallel decrease the total resistance
Household appliances like fans and refrigerators use electrical energy
formulas: Resistance in series → R = R1 + R2 + R3
exam_tips: Remember the definitions of current, potential difference, and resistance
Practice numerical problems using Ohm’s law
Understand differences between series and parallel combinations
Learn formulas for calculating electric power and electrical energy
Draw and label circuit diagrams clearly in exams
